insured.name_and_occupation: Michael Thompson – Owner, Thompson Family Grocery (small independent supermarket)
insured.address:
Thompson Family Grocery
1248 Oakridge Avenue
Springfield, IL 62704

description_of_loss.place_where_loss_damage_occurred: Front produce aisle and adjacent entrance area, Thompson Family Grocery, 1248 Oakridge Avenue, Springfield, IL 62704
description_of_loss.state_exactly_how_the_loss_damage_occurr:
At approximately 3:40 PM on 2025-09-18, a customer, later identified as Ms. Laura Jenkins, slipped and fell in the front produce aisle near the store entrance. Earlier that afternoon, around 3:15 PM, another customer had picked up a bag of red seedless grapes from the refrigerated display. According to our in-store security footage (which I have preserved and will provide upon request), a few loose grapes fell from the bag onto the polished tile floor and were not immediately noticed by staff.

Our produce clerk, Daniel Ruiz, had been restocking apples and oranges on the opposite side of the same aisle. At about 3:30 PM, he was called to assist at the front register due to a brief rush of customers. During that time, no one from staff performed a spot check of the produce aisle floor, and the wet floor caution sign that is usually placed near the entrance when there is a spill was not out because the floor had been dry earlier and no recent spills had been reported.

At around 3:39 PM, Ms. Jenkins entered the store with a reusable shopping bag and walked directly toward the produce section. The security footage shows her pushing a shopping cart and looking to her left toward the apples display while walking forward. As she reached the area where the grapes had fallen, her right foot stepped directly onto one of the loose grapes, causing her to lose her footing. Her right leg slid forward, and she fell backward, landing heavily on her lower back and left elbow. One or two additional grapes were crushed under her shoe, making the immediate area slippery.

The fall was loud enough that both the cashier at Register 1 (Emily Carter) and our produce clerk (Daniel), who had just stepped away from the register, heard it and immediately came over. Emily pressed the in-store assistance button to alert other staff. Daniel and I (I was in the small office just off the main floor) responded within about 30–60 seconds. When we arrived, Ms. Jenkins was on the floor on her back, conscious but visibly shaken and in pain. She stated more than once, "I slipped on something—my back really hurts." Daniel noticed the squashed grapes near her feet and immediately grabbed paper towels and a caution cone from the cleaning closet near the dairy section to mark off the area and prevent further incidents.

I knelt beside Ms. Jenkins, asked her if she could move her arms and legs, and if she felt any numbness or tingling. She said her lower back hurt badly and that her left elbow and hip were also very sore, but she could wiggle her toes and fingers. I asked if she wanted us to call an ambulance; she hesitated at first but then agreed, saying the pain was getting worse. I asked Emily to call 911 while I stayed with Ms. Jenkins, trying to keep her calm and advising her not to try to stand up until the paramedics arrived.

During this time, I also asked witnesses to remain in the area so I could take down their names and contact information after Ms. Jenkins was attended to. Two customers, Mr. Robert Lewis and Ms. Angela Price, reported seeing her slip and fall. Mr. Lewis specifically mentioned that he had noticed "a couple of grapes on the floor" just before she slipped but did not have a chance to alert anyone before the incident happened. Ms. Price confirmed that she saw Ms. Jenkins’s foot slide on something small and round.

The paramedics arrived approximately 10–12 minutes after the 911 call. They examined Ms. Jenkins on the floor, asked her about her pain level, and carefully helped her onto a stretcher. They placed a soft brace around her lower back and elbow and transported her to Springfield Memorial Hospital for further evaluation. I provided the paramedics with Ms. Jenkins’s name (as she gave it to me) and confirmed what had happened to the best of my knowledge. After they left, we performed a more thorough cleanup of the area, including mopping and placing a wet floor sign until the floor was completely dry.

Immediately after the incident, I completed an internal incident report, printed and saved the relevant portion of the CCTV footage, and instructed staff to perform more frequent floor checks in the produce aisle, especially around the grape display. I also spoke briefly with Ms. Jenkins’s husband on the phone later that evening, after he called the store to ask for a copy of the incident report, and I informed him that our public liability insurer would be notified. The loss appears to have arisen from loose grapes on the floor which were not spotted and removed quickly enough, and from Ms. Jenkins stepping on one of those grapes while walking into the store.

police.name_of_police_station_and_case_referenc: Springfield Police Department – Non-emergency call logged only, no formal case number provided at time of report. Dispatcher advised that, since EMS had already transported the injured party and there was no criminal activity suspected, a formal police report was not strictly necessary. I noted the date/time of the call: 2025-09-18 at approximately 16:05. N/A for formal case reference number.

property_damage.name_and_address_of_owner: N/A – No third-party property (other than the claimant’s personal items) was damaged. Store property (floor tiles, produce display) belongs to Thompson Family Grocery, 1248 Oakridge Avenue, Springfield, IL 62704.
property_damage.description_of_damage_to_property: There was no significant damage to third-party property. A few loose grapes were crushed on the tile floor and disposed of during cleanup. The store’s floor tiles and fixtures were not broken or permanently damaged. The customer’s personal items (a reusable shopping bag and its contents) did not appear visibly damaged at the time; she had only just entered and had not yet placed items in her cart. No other customers’ property was affected. Therefore, this claim is being submitted primarily in respect of potential bodily injury to the customer rather than property damage.

personal_injuries.address_of_injured_person:
Laura M. Jenkins
2067 Brookstone Lane
Springfield, IL 62711
personal_injuries.details_of_injuries:
At the time of the incident, Ms. Jenkins reported acute pain in her lower back (lumbar area), left hip, and left elbow. She stated that when she slipped on the grapes, her right foot went forward while the rest of her body fell backward, causing her to land heavily on her buttocks and lower back, with her left elbow striking the floor as she tried to brace the fall. Immediately after the fall, she was able to speak clearly, appeared alert and oriented, and could move all her limbs, but she described the pain in her lower back as "sharp" and "throbbing" and rated it around 7 or 8 out of 10 when asked by the responding paramedics.

Visually, I observed some initial redness around her left elbow and hip area, and she mentioned that her back felt "stiff" and that any attempt to twist or sit up increased her pain. The paramedics performed a brief on-site assessment, checking her spinal alignment, asking her about any numbness or tingling in her legs, and confirming that she did not hit her head and had not lost consciousness. They noted tenderness along the lumbar spine and left hip region and decided to transport her to Springfield Memorial Hospital for further evaluation, including imaging (X-rays or possibly an MRI) to rule out fractures or severe soft tissue injury.

Later that evening, Ms. Jenkins’s husband called the store and mentioned that the doctors were concerned about a possible lumbar strain and bruising to her left hip and elbow. He did not report any fractures at that time but said they were still waiting on final imaging results. He indicated that she was prescribed pain medication and advised to rest and avoid heavy lifting for at least several days. It is possible that she may have sustained a soft tissue injury, such as a lumbar sprain/strain or deep contusions, and she may require follow-up care with a primary care physician or physical therapist depending on how her symptoms progress.

I have not independently verified her final medical diagnosis or records, but based on her complaints and the paramedics’ initial assessment, the primary injuries appear to involve the lower back (lumbar strain or soft tissue injury), as well as contusions (bruising) to the left hip and left elbow resulting directly from the slip-and-fall incident in our store.

relationship.relationship_details_service_tenant_rela: Ms. Jenkins is not in our service, is not our tenant, and is not related to me or any member of my immediate family to the best of my knowledge. She is a regular retail customer of Thompson Family Grocery. I recognize her as someone who shops at our store roughly once or twice a month, often in the late afternoons or early evenings, but we have no relationship beyond that of store owner and customer. She does not work for us, does not rent any property from us, and we do not have any personal, familial, or business relationship outside of her occasional patronage of the store.

claim.details_of_claim_made_against_you:
On 2025-09-22, four days after the incident, I received a phone call from Ms. Jenkins’s husband, Mark Jenkins, informing me that they had consulted with a local attorney, and that they would likely be pursuing a claim for medical expenses and possibly for pain and suffering related to the injuries Ms. Jenkins sustained in our store. He requested a copy of our internal incident report and asked for the details of our public liability insurance provider.

On 2025-09-24, I received a formal letter from the law office of Carter & Reynolds, Attorneys at Law, located at 510 East Monroe Street, Springfield, IL 62701, representing Ms. Laura M. Jenkins. In that letter, Attorney Sarah Reynolds stated that Ms. Jenkins is alleging that our store failed to maintain a safe environment for customers by not promptly identifying and cleaning up the loose grapes in the produce aisle and by not placing adequate warning signage in the area. The letter indicates that Ms. Jenkins is claiming:

1. Reimbursement of medical expenses incurred to date (including emergency transport by ambulance, emergency room evaluation, diagnostic imaging, and follow-up visits),
2. Reimbursement for any future medical treatment reasonably related to the injuries sustained in the fall,
3. Compensation for pain, suffering, and loss of enjoyment of life due to ongoing back pain and mobility limitations,
4. Reimbursement for lost wages for any days she is unable to work as a result of the injuries.

The letter from Carter & Reynolds requests that I forward the correspondence to our liability insurer and that all further communication regarding settlement or liability determination be directed through their office. I have retained the original letter and provided a scanned copy to our broker. The letter does not specify an exact monetary amount at this stage but notes that a detailed demand will be issued once Ms. Jenkins’s course of treatment and prognosis are clearer.

I have not made any admission of liability to Ms. Jenkins or her representatives. I have acknowledged that the incident occurred and that we are cooperating with our insurance provider’s investigation. I have also preserved the relevant CCTV footage and the internal incident report for review by the insurer or any appointed loss adjuster or investigator. Copies of the attorney’s letter and our internal incident report can be provided to Southsure or its agents upon request.
